flashcards.front: What is a self-referential structure in C?
flashcards.back: A structure that contains a pointer to another variable of the same structure type. For chains, this is 'struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; };' which allows nodes to link to each other.

flashcards.front: How do you define a node using typedef for a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: typedef struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; } Node; -- This allows writing 'Node *head' instead of 'struct node *head' throughout the code.

flashcards.front: What is the correct way to allocate a new node with malloc in C?
flashcards.back: Node *newNode = (Node *)malloc(sizeof(Node)); -- Must include: type cast to Node*, sizeof(Node) for memory size, and NULL check after allocation.

flashcards.front: Why must you check for NULL after malloc?
flashcards.back: When malloc fails (system out of heap space), it returns NULL. Dereferencing a NULL pointer causes a segmentation fault (program crash). Always check: if (newNode == NULL) { printf("Memory allocation failed\n"); exit(1); }

flashcards.front: What is the role of the head pointer in a linked list?
flashcards.back: The head pointer stores the memory address of the first node. It is the gateway to the entire chain — if you lose the head pointer, the entire chain becomes inaccessible (memory leak). Functions that modify head must return the new head value.

flashcards.front: How does NULL indicate the end of a linked list?
flashcards.back: The last node's next pointer is set to NULL, which represents 'no address' or 'end of chain'. While traversing, you check 'while (current != NULL)' — when current becomes NULL, you've reached the end.

flashcards.front: Compare time complexity: inserting at beginning vs end of a linked list
flashcards.back: Insert at beginning: O(1) — one pointer assignment, no traversal needed. Insert at end: O(n) — must traverse all n nodes to find the last node before linking.

flashcards.front: How do you reverse a linked list using three pointers?
flashcards.back: Use prev, current, and next: while (current != NULL) { next = current->next; current->next = prev; prev = current; current = next; } return prev; -- This reverses links one at a time until prev becomes the new head.

flashcards.front: How do you properly free an entire linked list without losing nodes?
flashcards.back: While (current != NULL) { nextNode = current->next; free(current); current = nextNode; } -- Must save nextNode BEFORE freeing current, otherwise the rest of the chain is lost.

flashcards.front: Compare arrays vs linked lists: memory layout and access by index
flashcards.back: Arrays use contiguous memory (adjacent locations), allowing O(1) random access by index. Linked lists use scattered heap memory, requiring O(n) traversal to access the k-th element since nodes are connected via pointers.
